responses_by_mode:
Drops on green leaves,
a river is born from sky.
Dripping eaves echo.

Drops on green leaves,
puddles mirror clouds above.
Silence after storm.

Drops on tin roofs,
a river is born from sky.
Silence after storm.

Drops on warm stone,
the world sighs and glistens.
Frogs begin to sing.

Drops on green leaves,
the world sighs and glistens.
Petrichor rises.

Recipe: Monsoon Broth
Ingredients: 3 cups vegetable stock, sliced mushrooms, bay leaves.
Simmer on low for 30 minutes until golden.

Recipe: Drizzle Chowder
Ingredients: 3 cups vegetable stock, cubed carrots, fresh rosemary.
Simmer on low for two hours until bubbling.

Recipe: Rainy Day Soup
Ingredients: 1 quart bone broth, diced potatoes, bay leaves.
Simmer on low for two hours until fragrant.

Recipe: Storm Cloud Stew
Ingredients: 4 cups miso broth, diced potatoes, fresh rosemary.
Simmer on low for 30 minutes until bubbling.

Recipe: Drizzle Chowder
Ingredients: 1 quart bone broth, cubed carrots, bay leaves.
Simmer on low for an hour until fragrant.
Rain forms when water vapor reaches its dew point in rising air. A typical raindrop is about 2 millimeters wide and falls at roughly 10 meters per second.
Rain forms when water vapor reaches its dew point in rising air. A typical raindrop is roughly 0.5 to 4 mm across and falls at terminal velocity of 5-9 m/s.
Rain forms when water vapor reaches its dew point in rising air. A typical raindrop is about 2 millimeters wide and falls at roughly 10 meters per second.
Rain forms when water vapor aggregates in cumulonimbus clouds. A typical raindrop is about 2 millimeters wide and falls at terminal velocity of 5-9 m/s.
Rain forms when water vapor condenses around dust particles. A typical raindrop is roughly 0.5 to 4 mm across and falls at approximately 9 m/s.
I remember the summer rain in Kyoto. I was sitting in a cafe by the window and everything turned silver and quiet. It was peaceful.
I remember the autumn downpour in Seattle. I was standing under an awning and strangers huddled together laughing. It was peaceful.
I remember the autumn downpour in Seattle. I was running to catch a bus and strangers huddled together laughing. It was magical.
I remember the summer rain in Kyoto. I was running to catch a bus and the smell of wet earth filled the air. It was strangely beautiful.
I remember the monsoon in Mumbai. I was walking without an umbrella and the smell of wet earth filled the air. It was peaceful.

Top 5 facts about rain:
1. The smell of rain is called petrichor
2. Mawsynram, India receives 467 inches of rain per year
3. Raindrops can fall at up to 22 mph
4. Acid rain was first identified in 1852
5. Rain contains dissolved nitrogen that fertilizes soil

Top 5 facts about rain:
1. A single thunderstorm can drop 500 million gallons of water
2. Antarctica is technically the driest continent
3. Raindrops are not teardrop-shaped; they are flattened spheres
4. Rain on Venus is made of sulfuric acid
5. Phantom rain evaporates before hitting the ground

Top 5 facts about rain:
1. The smell of rain is called petrichor
2. Antarctica is technically the driest continent
3. It takes about 10 minutes for a raindrop to reach the ground
4. Rain on Venus is made of sulfuric acid
5. Red rain fell in Kerala, India in 2001

Top 5 facts about rain:
1. The smell of rain is called petrichor
2. Mawsynram, India receives 467 inches of rain per year
3. Raindrops can fall at up to 22 mph
4. Diamond rain may fall on Neptune
5. Phantom rain evaporates before hitting the ground

Top 5 facts about rain:
1. Rain is recycled water that has evaporated from oceans and lakes
2. The wettest place on Earth gets over 11 meters of rain annually
3. The largest raindrops ever recorded were 8.8 mm across
4. Rain on Venus is made of sulfuric acid
5. Rain contains dissolved nitrogen that fertilizes soil

"Looks like rain again." asked Sarah.
"I love the sound of it." said the shopkeeper, peering out the window.

"Do you think it will rain?" asked Sarah.
"The forecast says so." said the shopkeeper, peering out the window.

"Do you think it will rain?" asked Sarah.
"I hope not, I left my coat." said the shopkeeper, holding out a hand to feel the air.

"Can you hear that thunder?" asked the old woman.
"I hope not, I left my coat." answered her father, glancing at the darkening sky.

"Did you bring an umbrella?" asked Sarah.
"I hope not, I left my coat." said the shopkeeper, glancing at the darkening sky.
Rain is perhaps the most democratic force in nature. It treats every surface the same. We are reminded of our smallness when the sky opens.
Rain is perhaps the most democratic force in nature. It falls on the rich and poor alike. We are reminded of our smallness when the sky opens.
Rain is perhaps the most honest force in nature. It does not discriminate between palace and gutter. Each drop is both an ending and a beginning.
Rain is perhaps the most democratic force in nature. It treats every surface the same. In rain, we see the cycle that sustains all life.
Rain is perhaps the most honest force in nature. It falls on the rich and poor alike. There is something humbling about standing in a downpour.

Oh, the rain comes falling down,
soaking through my coat and evening gown.
The gutters overflow and church bells ring.

Oh, the rain comes falling down,
soaking through my coat and evening gown.
The flowers bow their heads accepting spring.

Oh, the rain comes rolling down,
drumming on the rooftops of the town.
The children splash in puddles everything.

Oh, the rain comes tumbling down,
drumming on the rooftops of the town.
The gutters overflow and church bells ring.

Oh, the rain comes tumbling down,
turning every field to shining brown.
The children splash in puddles everything.
Let R ~ Poisson(lambda=2.1) model daily rainfall events. The expected seasonal total is E[R] * 90 = 36.4 mm. Variance scales linearly: Var(sum) = n * lambda.
Let R ~ Poisson(lambda=2.1) model daily rainfall events. The expected seasonal total is E[R] * 90 = 141 mm. Variance scales linearly: Var(sum) = N * sigma^2.
Let R ~ Poisson(lambda=5.5) model daily rainfall events. The expected monthly accumulation is E[R] * 365 = 141 mm. Variance scales linearly: Var(sum) = N * sigma^2.
Let R ~ Poisson(lambda=5.5) model daily rainfall events. The expected seasonal total is E[R] * 7 = 36.4 mm. Variance scales linearly: Var(sum) = n * lambda.
Let R ~ Poisson(lambda=4.7) model daily rainfall events. The expected weekly runoff is E[R] * 90 = 36.4 mm. Variance scales linearly: Var(sum) = n * Var(R).
The city was a mirror and the rain was the whisper that woke it. Every window received the same baptism.
The city was a drum and the rain was the truth. Every window surrendered to the rhythm.
The city was a canvas and the rain was the truth. Every leaf told a different story.
The city was a mirror and the rain was the drummer. Every window received the same baptism.
The city was a mirror and the rain was the whisper that woke it. Every leaf became an instrument.
In 1970, the Bhola cyclone devastated East Pakistan with torrential rain. The Dust Bowl of the 1930s was caused partly by the absence of rain.
In 1931, the Yangtze River floods killed an estimated 3.7 million people. Rainfall patterns have shaped the rise and fall of civilizations.
In 2005, Hurricane Katrina brought record rainfall to the Gulf Coast. Monsoon failures triggered famines across South Asia for centuries.
In 1970, the Bhola cyclone devastated East Pakistan with torrential rain. Rainfall patterns have shaped the rise and fall of civilizations.

Dear Dr. Chen,
I am writing to express concern about drainage infrastructure in the downtown corridor. The community has noticed significant changes.
Sincerely, R. Stormfield

Dear City Council,
I am writing to express concern about drainage infrastructure in the downtown corridor. Recent measurements show alarming trends.
Sincerely, Dr. A. Marsh

Dear Professor Hawkins,
I am writing to request funding for rainfall research in the downtown corridor. The data from last quarter suggests a 30% increase.
Sincerely, Dr. A. Marsh

I am writing to express concern about drainage infrastructure in the downtown corridor. Recent measurements show alarming trends.
Sincerely, R. Stormfield

Once upon a time, in a cozy little town, a little cloud named Nimbus wanted to make the flowers grow. So it floated down from the sky and the world sparkled.
Once upon a time, in a faraway kingdom, a small raindrop named Drizzle wanted to help the thirsty trees. So it floated down from the sky and everyone danced outside.
Once upon a time, in a faraway kingdom, a shy rainbow named Nimbus wanted to help the thirsty trees. So it gathered all its friends and everyone danced outside.
Once upon a time, in a faraway kingdom, a shy rainbow named Drizzle wanted to fill up the village pond. So it jumped from cloud to cloud and the ducks quacked happily.
Once upon a time, in a cozy little town, a shy rainbow named Splashy wanted to wash the dusty streets. So it took a deep breath and let go and the world sparkled.
